title: 6 1 Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
body:
6.1 EIGENVALUES AND EIGENVECTORS


Given a square matrix A, a non-zero vector v is said to be an eigenvector of A if

                                          Av = λv

for some λ ∈ R. The number λ is referred to as the eigenvalue of A associated with v.

We first find eigenvalues by solving the characteristic equation det(A − λI) = 0. The
eigenvectors are then found via row reduction and back substitution. A useful check
to make sure you have all the eigenvalues is to use the fact that Σ(eigenvalues) = Trace(A).

The zero vector is never an eigenvector but it is OK to have a zero eigenvalue.

If an n × n matrix A has n linearly independent eigenvectors and P is the matrix of
eigenvectors (i.e., the eigenvectors form the columns of the matrix P ), then

                                       P −1 AP = D

where D is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues. The ordering of the eigenvalues in
D must match the ordering of the eigenvectors in P . This process is referred to as
diagonalisation.



Square matrices transform vectors, stretching them and rotating them in space. As an
example, consider what happens when the following matrix multiplies a vector:
                                          
                                 1 4        1
                                                 =
                                −3 9        0




But now consider                                         
                                    1 4         2
                                                        =
                                   −3 9         3




Observe that
          A  simply makes this vector 7 times as long — there is no rotation. We say
           2
that v =        is an eigenvector of A with associated eigenvalue λ = 7.
           3


                                            1
 More generally, an eigenvector v is a special vector that, when multiplied by A, simply
gives back v multiplied by some number λ,

                                  Av = λv,       (v 6= 0).

The eigenvalue λ can be greater than one (the vector v is lengthened), less than one (the
vector v is shortened), negative (the direction of v is reversed) or even zero (the vector v
has zero length). However, the vector v must be non-zero to be an eigenvector.

To find the eigenvectors and eigenvalues of a matrix A we rewrite the above equation as

                         Av − λv = Av − λIv = (A − λI)v = 0.

Since v 6= 0 then the matrix (A − λI) must be non-invertible. This means that

                                     det(A − λI) = 0.

This is called the characteristic equation and it is a polynomial equation for the eigen-
value λ. A 2 × 2 matrix will have a quadratic characteristic equation and a 3 × 3 matrix
will have a cubic characteristic equation. Once you have the eigenvalues you can then
find the eigenvectors by solving (A − λI)v = 0 using row reduction.
                                                                        
                                                                   1 4
Example 1 Find all the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A =                 and hence
                                                                  −3 9
find an invertible matrix P and a diagonal matrix D such that P −1 AP = D.




                                             2
                                         
                2 2                  3 0
    F P =                 , D=                 F
                1 3                  0 7

3
                                                                     
                                                             −2 2 −3
Example 2 Find all the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A =  2  1 −6  and
                                                             −1 −2 0
hence diagonalise A.


We begin by finding the eigenvalues of A. In this case the matrix is 3 × 3 so the char-
acteristic polynomial will be a cubic equation. These are difficult to solve so we need to
work carefully through the determinant to see if we can find a common factor.




The eigenvalues are λ = −3, −3, 5. As a check we use the fact that the sum of eigenvectors
must be the same as the trace of A (the sum of the diagonal components of A):

                            −3 − 3 + 5 = −2 + 1 + 0 = −1.
Note that we have a double eigenvalue λ = −3. However, this does not mean that we
will be short one eigenvector. In fact, there will be two eigenvectors associated with the
eigenvalue λ = −3.

                                            4
 Let’s now find the eigenvectors:




                                   5
 Lastly we form the matrix of eigenvectors P and the matrix of eigenvalues D, being careful
to make sure that the order of the eigenvectors matches the order of the eigenvalues.




                                                             
                                        −1 −2 3          5 0  0
                                F P =  −2 1 0  , D =  0 −3 0                         F
                                         1 0 1           0 0 −3


What is happening with the process of diagonalisation? Remember that a square matrix
A will transform a vector, but it can only stretch or shrink its eigenvector by a factor λ. So
if we put the eigenvectors in the columns of a matrix P and then multiply by the matrix
A, the result will be that each column will simply be multiplied by its corresponding
eigenvalue
                                                                  
                                     −1               −2               3 
              AP = P D = 5 ×  −2  , −3 ×  1  , −3 ×  0  .
                                       1                0               1
                                                                            

We can then multiply on the right by P −1 on each side of the equation to get

                                        P −1 AP = D.

What this does is transform the matrix A to a special basis where it takes on its simplest
form, stretching or shrinking its eigenvectors in each direction.

Although the diagonal matrix D is the simplest form of A, it is not unique. If we re-
arrange the columns of the matrix P so that the eigenvectors are in a different order, the
corresponding eigenvectors in D will also be re-ordered. The order of eigenvalues in D
always has to match the order of the eigenvectors in P . The matrix P is also not unique,
because each eigenvector is only defined up to a multiplicative constant.
